---
title: Hypothesis Tests
description: Evidence against a baseline claim.
---

::: {.learning-objectives}
### Learning Objectives

- Define null and alternative hypotheses.
- Interpret p-values cautiously.
- Connect tests to uncertainty.
:::

A hypothesis test asks whether observed data are surprising under a baseline
claim called the null hypothesis.

::: {.common-mistake}
### Misreading p-values

A p-value is not the probability that the null hypothesis is true.
:::

::: {.practice-box}
1. What is a null hypothesis?
2. What does a small p-value suggest?
3. Why does statistical significance not always mean practical importance?
:::

## Solutions

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## Solution 1

Answer: the baseline claim tested against the data.

It often represents "no effect", "no difference", or an existing assumption.
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## Solution 2

Answer: the observed data would be unlikely if the null hypothesis were true.

That is evidence against the null, not proof that the alternative is true.
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## Solution 3

Answer: a tiny effect can be statistically significant in a large sample but
still too small to matter in practice.
:::
